sentences.text: My name is Albert Tausinger.
Yes?
My parents--
Spell?
T-A-U-S-I-N-G-E-R.
Now I am nearly 85.
I was born in-- now it called is [?
Lodno-- ?] in Transylvania.
It was the Austro-Hungarian monarchy.
Yes.
I am the youngest from seven.
I have two-- four brothers and two sisters.
Now I am the last one alive.
My father works in timber-- the timber industry.
So he ran around all the Austro-Hungarian monarchy.
It happens that from the seven children after 1918, the war-- end of the first war, we are born in five different countries.
Now, my parents never have been abroad-- always in Austro-Hungarian monarchy.
In 1918, the Austro-Hungarian monarchy split, and it was five independent countries.
It was the Czechoslovakia, Austria, Hungarian, Rumania, Poland, and Prussia.
So we are all born around-- around-- only in Austro-Hungarian monarchy.
My parents never ever was abroad.
They never had a passport.
Went together.
Went together up-- up to 1914.
In 1914, when broke out the war, we evacuated back inland to Hungary-- to the north Hungary.
After [? 18th, ?] it was Czechoslovakia.
We brought [? up ?] back except two brothers.
The one brother was in-- Walter was in the army, in the First World War.
And in 1918, we have been in Czechoslovak.
The border was Czechoslovakia.
Now, origin, my father-- what I heard.
I don't have documents.
They come from Spain.
They are Sephardins.
My mother, she comes from Galicia.
She was a ultra Orthodox Jewish-- a kohanim.
So in the family about the religion was a little bit difficult.
The mother was strong religious.
The father and the boys wasn't.
I had my bar mitzvah [? though ?] I wasn't religious.
Sorry?
They met when my father works in Galicia by the timber.
Some people-- he met there my mother.
What happens?
He married this.
And when they have been under chuppah, the Orthodox Jewish, they want the mother cut the hair, to take a sheitel.
So my father said, the hair are very nice.
I don't like to cut it.
When you cut the hair, I go out from the chuppah.
So my mother had the hair still.
No.
No.
At home, in the house, was strong kosher.
Now, outside the house we eat everything.
Now, ?] at home was strong kosher.
Strong Friday evening, Erev Shabbos.
Strong Yom Kippur, strong New Year, Rosh Hashanah.
Normal kosher meal.
We eat.
Nobody say the brokhe.
Nobody know it.
So it was difficult to say.
Religious, [? pro ?] [? forma. ?] Strong religion at home between-- in the house.
We are going in the synagogue twice a year plus in my bar mitzvah.
Nobody goes to the synagogue.
So, in Central Europe, Austro-Hungarian, where we lived was only two Jewish groups.
It was the Neolog group-- we belonged to them-- or-- and the Orthodox.
No, ?] there was not a Orthodox like here-- ultra Orthodox.
They were Orthodox.
They had a small shul, and there was a Neolog synagogue.
And did your mother go to synagogue?
The mother goes to the synagogue.
I can't remember every week or not.
No.
She-- she was religious.
At home was, again, difficult.
My mother spoke only German from home.
She spoke Yiddish too-- German and Yiddish.
So we travel around around.
So my mother understand Hungarian.
My older brother and sister, they go to the-- in Hungarian school and spoke Hungarian.
Now, the mother-- when the brother, sister, talk to the mother in Hungarian, the mother always answer in German.
So the-- my oldest brother, sister, they understand the German.
They don't speak German.
German speak only I and my sister, a younger sister.
In 1918, I started the school one month in Hungarian.
It was in September 1918.
In October 1918, it was Czechoslovakia, independent Czechoslovakia.
So I started again in the Slovak language.
So my mother, again, the same trouble.
She understood our Slovak.
Now ?] always we talk in German.
So actually, I was growing up in German language.
So my German is very well.
It is-- my English is very bad.
I am sorry.
Not at all.
Not at all.
And your father-- what did your father speak?
My father, he spoke Hungarian.
He spoke German and Slovak.
Yes.
What bitte?
What I remember?
My father, what I remember him, he was always trouble with his leg.
Before the First World War, in the-- on the woods, he fall in the winter in water.
And [? since then, ?] he had frozen.
And [? since then, ?] he had always trouble with his leg.
So he goes only with a stick.
I don't remember him to go normal without a stick.
I don't remember my parents-- parents-- there are all only grey hair.
The mother and the father of what I remember, only grey-haired.
Sorry?
I don't understand.
So I told you.
The mother was-- comes from an ultra Orthodox Jewish.
Special-- I was the youngest from seven.
So I was a young boy.
So I-- my mother give me everything.
The oldest brother, sister, they was [? out ?] from house away.
And the first what I have-- not a trouble with my mother or the mother with me.
When my sister, she was married and have a boy, a child-- [? the ?] [?
Hermonie. ?] In this time, I was 10 years old.
And the nephew was born.
So what happens?
It was the first grandchild.
And my mother always look about the grandchild while my sister was working.
So I-- I was afraid I don't like my grandson-- my nephew.
You know?
Now after, it was all right.
After it started the other nephews and nieces.
Tell me a bit about your brothers and sisters.
My brothers and sisters-- the oldest brother was lucky.
He was on the front in 1918, somewhere in Italy.
He got a holiday and come home in 1918 [INAUDIBLE].
My second brother, he was a little bit [? worst. ?] He was in the army and was a prisoner of war in Russia.
He married there a Russian Orthodox girl and come back in 1922.
So it was the first time in 1922 that the family was all together.
I don't remember-- I lost absolutely-- when married my oldest brother.
I don't remember when married my older sister.
I lost the time.
Did you get on well with your brothers and sisters?
I get very well.
No, ?] it was the trouble.
I was a 10-year-old youngster, and my brother was nearly 20.
It was a different-- nearly a generation.
So they have grown up people, and I was a child always.
So it-- contact between my brother and-- and the older sister was difficult.
The age different.
The sister was 14 years older.
When I was 10 years old, my sister was 24 years old.
She had a child.
I was still a child.
Close I was and near to my second sister.
He ?] was here in Britain, in London.
She died here.
So with her, we were close.
It was only four years age different.
What?
Katiza, ?] Katerina.
School?
No, ?] I-- I started-- I started in 1918 when I was six years old in the Austro-Hungarian monarchy-- started the school at six years.
So I started in September.
It was still Austro-Hungarian monarchy in a Hungarian school.
In 1918, it was independent Czechoslovakia.
So I started in a Slovak school.
It was so in Zilina where we live.
There was three elementary schools.
It was a state school, it was a Catholic school, and it was a Jewish school.
Well, I started in the Jewish school, in Slovak in German.
It was interesting.
In the Jewish school in Zilina, was nearly the half the children were German.
They don't go in the state school.
They don't go in the Catholic school.
They go to the Jewish school where they was teaching German too.
No.
No.
Wasn't Jewish.
German.
Origin German they were in Zilina.
There were a lot of-- I don't know how say English-- volksdeutsche.
German who lives not in Germany.
He lives around Europe, groups.
So there was children from the so-called volksdeutsche.
They go to the Jewish school.
And relation, actually, I must tell you, between the German-- the volksdeutsche-- and the Jewish in Zilina was very nice, very good.
And they prefer to be friend with the Jewish while they can talk always German.
Jewish.
I had a lot Jewish.
And I have friends German too.
We go always-- I like skiing.
So we go out for skiing.
You can put on the skis on the front of the house.
There was started to snow November, December, up to April.
And we go on the mountains.
And the second my sport was [? canoistic. ?] And there were a lot-- some Slovaks.
No, ?] mostly German-speaking people.
Jewish and German and few Slovak.
The house?
In 1918, when we run away from the-- where my father worked in the timber-- to Zilina, it was outside.
It was-- we lived in a flat without electricity, 1918.
It was a two-room flat, and we had been-- I don't remember-- five, six people.
So in one room we sleep four, five children, the parent.
After, I don't remember the-- the year.
We found a house.
It was-- yes.
In this where we live was no toilet, no running water.
We-- we have the flat on the first floor.
And the toilet, you must run down to the garden.
No.
I don't remember.
It was in 1920, '21?
It was a widower-- a widow.
She moved to Vienna, and we rent from her the house.
So it was started-- we started to live normally with running water, with toilet.
And there was three or four rooms.
And this house was just near Zilina?
No.
It was in Zilina.
Zilina.
It was where my father works in the timber.
And local people, they don't like the Jewish.
So we run in the town.
That ?] wasn't-- no exist for my father.
Sorry.
Again?
Not only my father.
They don't like the Jewish, the Slovak.
This was a small village--
With the timber factory.
No, no, no, no, no.
They don't hurt.
They take everything from us.
They come and take everything what you find in the-- in the house.
It was the-- it wasn't the anti-Semitism.
It [? was ?] the things what you take away from us.
Mostly.
Sure.
How old I was?
Five and a half year old.
Six-- not six.
It was in 1918 when the war finished.
No.
Mostly day.
All I remember.
It's 80 years ago.
As child in Zilina, I don't feel anti-Semitism.
Zilina was a town-- how you call it-- a free Austro-Hungarian town.
It was a small town.
It was 17,000, 18,000 inhabitants.
There was Jewish, Hungarian-- so-called-- to be exact, it was so-called Hungarian Slovak.
They were Slovak.
[INAUDIBLE] all speak Hungarian.
And volksdeutsche, the German.
So, these three groups.
And these three groups, they hold nice together.
It wasn't anti-Semitism.
Anti-Semitism I started, to be honest, after 1918.
What happens?
The Slovaks, the so-called Slovaks, they don't have Slovak intelligence.
The intelligence-- in this time in, the [?
Slovakie ?] was Hungarian-speaking.
There ?] was nationality Slovaks, you know?
No, ?] speaks Hungarian, not Slovak.
In the '20s, the Czechs-- the other part of Czechoslovakia-- they sent the intelligent-- intelligence to [?
Slovakie. ?] And the business-- the shops and this-- was in the hand mostly 90% in Jewish.
The Jewish had the-- the business.
So the Czechs sent teachers and workers and everything.
Now, to take the shops, they started the anti-Semitisms.
In the [?
Slovakie, ?] especially in Zilina, the anti-Semitism started with the Czechs, not with the Slovaks.
What do you remember about anti-Semitism in Zilina as a child?
Am ?] a child?
I-- to-- only the anti-Semitism was the Czech youngsters what are the childrens who around my age.
They hold more together.
They don't like the-- the German don't like the Jewish.
They contact mostly with the-- with the Slovak, the Hungarian Slovaks.
Not the German and not the Jewish.
Jewish?
[INAUDIBLE] a child-- I was in the Maccabi.
It was a gymnastic and skiing.
It was a group Maccabi.
By myself.
My sister-- when I was 10, she was 14.
When I started with the 12, 14 years, she was a 20 years.
And she married, I think, after [INAUDIBLE] 17.
She married-- she was 18 years old when she married.
From my childhood, special was only what I liked and was very nice was the skiing and the [? canoistic. ?] I was a fanatic skier and [? canoeist ?] and tourist.
We walk around the mountains.
Zilina was-- is in the mountains.
Same the town was, I think, 600 meter over the sea.
No.
Mostly with friends.
Always with friends.
No by myself.
We like everything in this time.
We was always hungry.
Yes, always.
We eat normally in the morning coffee and bread.
And lunch and evening.
And actually it was between morning and lunch have always some coffee or bread with butter.
And after the lunch, 4 o'clock, again, we had a coffee or something.
No.
It was not this coffee what we drink here.
It was-- I don't know how it is.
After my first school, I go-- I finish the-- it was-- in Austro-Hungarian monarchy, was the first school, from 6 to 10.
Then was the middle school from 10 to 14, 15.
After my-- when I was 15, I go to the business academy.
It was a business school.
Yes.
